retrieved_docs.title: Clark's Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clark's rule is an equation used to calculate pediatric medication dosage based on the known weight of a patient and a known adult dose of medication to be used. Clark's rule equation is defined as the weight of the patient in pounds divided by the average standard weight of 150 pounds (68 kg) multiplied by the adult dose of a drug to obtain the pediatric medication dose, as is demonstrated below: (Weight* divided by 150 lbs.) x Adult Dose** = Pediatric Dosage (Weight*** divided by 68 kg) x Adult Dose** = Pediatric Dosage *Weight of pediatric patient in pounds (lbs.) **Adult dose is the recommended dosage for adult medication use ***Weight of pediatric patient in kilograms (kg)

retrieved_docs.title: Median -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The median is the middle value in a set of numbers and is equivalent to the 50th percentile. In other words, the median is the midpoint of a set of numbers, with half of the values less than the median and half above it. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Median -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: What should be done if the set contains an even number of values? In this case, the median is the average of the 2 middle numbers. To illustrate this principle, the second set of numbers (7, 3, 10, 2, 9, 2, 1, 4) should be considered. After arranging the set of numbers in ascending order, we get (1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 7, 9, 10). As the second set of numbers has 8 numbers, the 2 middle values are 3 and 4. The median is the average of these 2 numbers, which is calculated as follows: (3+4)/2 = 3.5 Notably, the median of 3.5 is different from the mean, or average, of the set of numbers, which is 4.75. This is calculated as follows: (1+2+2+3+4+7+9+10)/8 = 38/8 = 4.75

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Bioavailability -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bioavailability can be derived from an area under the curve (AUC) graph (Equation 3), which can be observed in the associated Figure 1 . [4] For clinical purposes, a conceptual understanding of an AUC graph is crucial. Equation 3: F = AUC for X route of administration ÷ AUC for IV administration

retrieved_docs.title: Mean -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The mean, which is also known as the average, is the total sum of values in a sample divided by the number of values in your sample. [1] For example, to figure out a grade at the end of a course, you calculate the mean of all of your test scores.  If you scored a 95%, 90%, 97%, and 92% on tests,  your mean test score would be:

retrieved_docs.title: Median -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A short example will help clarify this point. In the set of 7 numbers (8, 6, 9, 5, 8, 23, 4), the median is found by first sorting the numbers from lowest to highest (in ascending order). The sorted set becomes (4, 5, 6, 8, 8, 9, 23). The median is the middle number. As there are 7 numbers in the set, the fourth value, '8', is the median. Notably, the median differs from the mean. The mean is the "average" of the values in the set, calculated as follows: (4+5+6+8+8+9+23)/7 = 63/7 = 9

retrieved_docs.title: Mean -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Mathematically the mean is the sum of the values in a set divided by the number of values in the set.  For a given set of n values, one can add together all of the values in the set then divide by n to find the mean.

retrieved_docs.title: Clark's Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clark's rule is a known pediatric medication dosing rule described in the medical literature that utilizes the patient's weight to calculate medication dosage. Other equations that utilize pediatric weight to calculate medication dosing include Salisbury's rule, Penna's rule, and the Body Surface Area rule. Other methods besides Clark's rule that utilize pediatric age to calculate radiopharmaceutical medication dosages used in nuclear imaging include Young's rule, Webster's rule, and Fried's rule. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Young's Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Young's Rule is an equation used to calculate pediatric medication dosage based on patient age and the known recommended adult dose. The definition of Young's Rule is the age of the patient divided by the age added to twelve, all multiplied by the recommended adult dose. This formula appears below:

retrieved_docs.title: Young's Rule -- Definition/Introduction -- [Age / (Age + 12)] x Recommended Adult Dose = Pediatric Dose
retrieved_docs.content: Young's Rule can be applied quickly in situations where the current weight of the patient is unknown. Other approaches to pediatric dosing using age include the Webster and Fried rules. [1] The Clark Rule or the Body Surface Area Rule can be implemented if the weight of the patient is known. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Use Of The Child Pugh Score In Liver Disease -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalopathy: None = 1 point, Grade 1 and 2 = 2 points, Grade 3 and 4 = 3 points Ascites:  None = 1 point, slight = 2 points, moderate = 3 points Bilirubin: under 2 mg/ml = 1 point, 2 to 3 mg/ml = 2 points, over 3 mg/ml = 3 points Albumin: greater than 3.5mg/ml = 1 point, 2.8 to 3.5mg/ml = 2 points, less than 2.8mg/ml = 3 points Prothrombin Time* (sec prolonged): less than 4 sec = 1 point, 4 to 6 sec = 2 points, over 6 sec = 3 points *Frequently INR will be used as a substitute for PT, with INR under 1.7 = 1 point, INR 1.7 to 2.2 = 2 points, INR above 2.2 = 3 points The severity of cirrhosis: Child-Pugh A: 5 to 6 points Child-Pugh B: 7 to 9 points Child-Pugh C: 10 to 15 points

retrieved_docs.title: Six Sigma Method -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the define phase, problems and issues within the process are outlined from both the business and client perspectives. This may include launching a team, gathering information, planning the project, and setting goals. For example, a project may define a medical error the organization wants to address. During the measure phase, further narrowing of the project focus occurs, and baseline data are collected. In the analyze phase, data are examined to help identify the root cause of an issue and help remove inefficiencies.

retrieved_docs.content: The process of determining the sample SD of a data set is outlined in the example below, which will use the following data: 4, 5, 5, 5, 7, 8, 8, 8, 9, 10

retrieved_docs.title: Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The MMPI was developed in the 1930s and published through the University of Minnesota in 1942 by Stuart Hathaway and Charley McKinley using visitors of patients at the University of Minnesota hospital as a base sample in both theorizing constructs of psychiatric illness and fielding the instrument. Testing is administered through 567 true or false items using a booklet with an accompanying answer sheet. The responses are then hand-scored and plotted on an X-Y graph; a separate version is used for male and female respondents. [3] The X-axis is comprised of 14 scales. The first four ‘content scales’ judge the validity of the test attempt and include:

retrieved_docs.title: Revised Trauma Scale -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: When using the RTS to predict geriatric trauma mortality, the recommended cutoff value was 7.108, with a sensitivity of 97% and a specificity of 80%. [7] [10] There were statistically significant differences regarding the receiving operator characteristics (ROC) curve between ISS, [6] New Injury Severity Score (NISS), [11] , and the Trauma and Injury Severity Score (TRISS), [12] in predicting geriatric mortality. [10] The measurement tool that best predicted elderly mortality, based on the ROC, was the TRISS, [12] but the RTS was also an appropriate measurement tool to predict the same. [10] When patients transported via Helicopter Emergency Medical Services (HEMS) were assessed using the RTS, [7] the optimal cutoff score to predict trauma patients at risk for a major complication during a HEMS mission was 11.5. The researchers reported a sensitivity of 84% and a specificity of 90.5%, thereby validating the usefulness of the RTS as an appropriate assessment tool. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: ED50 -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There are two dose-response curve (DRC) forms: graded and quantal. Graded answers the question, ‘How much?’ whereas quantal DRC answers, ‘Yes or no?’ The ED50 may change depending on the question the clinician is trying to answer; using the recommended ED50 as a starting point is recommended, making adjustments based on the clinical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1983
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is classified within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), indicating that gestational length is a recognized medical category in this coding system. Within this framework, the specific gestational interval of “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is linked to the outcome of a “liveborn” infant, reflecting that infants born after completing 29 to 30 weeks of pregnancy can be alive at birth. Accordingly, the “liveborn” outcome is treated as a distinct subclass or child concept under the broader umbrella of gestational duration, illustrating how gestational age ranges and birth outcomes are organized together in clinical classification and patient‑care documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2578
retrieved_docs.content: Acute and unspecified renal failure is described as being related to and possibly synonymous with كلاء الكليون السفلي, indicating that the two terms may refer to the same clinical concept.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of
